title: Quiz: Quantum Algorithms for Network Analysis
description: Test your understanding of quantum approaches to network analysis

questions.question: PageRank assigns importance to nodes based on:

options.text: The number of outgoing links from the node
The structure of incoming links from other important nodes
The node's position in the graph
Random assignment

questions.explanation: PageRank assigns importance based on the random surfer model: a node is important if it receives links from other important nodes. The score is computed iteratively: pi = alpha * P^T * pi + (1-alpha)/n, where links from high-PageRank nodes contribute more.

questions.question: How can quantum walks be used to approximate PageRank?

options.text: By directly computing the transition matrix eigenvector
By evolving from uniform superposition and measuring probabilities
By encoding PageRank as a QUBO problem
PageRank cannot be computed with quantum walks

questions.explanation: Quantum walks starting from uniform superposition evolve according to the graph structure. Time-averaged measurement probabilities correlate with centrality measures including PageRank. The quantum walk mixing relates to the stationary distribution of related random walks.

questions.question: Spectral clustering for community detection uses:

options.text: Edge colors to partition the graph
Eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian to embed nodes
Random sampling of node pairs
Depth-first search traversal

questions.explanation: Spectral clustering computes the eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian (L = D - A), which provide a low-dimensional embedding of nodes. Nodes in the same community tend to have similar eigenvector coordinates, making clustering in this space effective.

questions.question: The modularity measure for community detection quantifies:

options.text: The number of communities in the network
The difference between actual and expected edge density within communities
The total number of edges in the network
The diameter of the network

questions.explanation: Modularity Q compares the fraction of edges within communities to the expected fraction under a random null model. Q = sum over communities of (actual within-community edges - expected). High modularity indicates strong community structure.

questions.question: The max-flow min-cut theorem states that:

options.text: Maximum flow equals minimum number of edges
Maximum s-t flow equals minimum s-t cut capacity
Minimum flow equals maximum cut
Flow and cut are independent quantities

questions.explanation: The max-flow min-cut theorem establishes that the maximum flow that can be sent from source s to sink t equals the minimum capacity of any s-t cut (partition separating s from t). This duality is fundamental to network flow algorithms.

questions.question: Quantum algorithms for network analysis provide exponential speedup for all network metrics.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Quantum speedups for network analysis are typically polynomial (often quadratic), not exponential. For PageRank, theoretical speedup is O(sqrt(n)) but with significant caveats. Many network problems have classical polynomial-time algorithms that are hard to beat asymptotically.

questions.question: Betweenness centrality of a node measures:

options.text: The number of neighbors the node has
The fraction of shortest paths that pass through the node
The average distance to all other nodes
The PageRank score of the node

questions.explanation: Betweenness centrality counts the fraction of all-pairs shortest paths that pass through a given node. Nodes with high betweenness are 'bridges' that control information flow. Classical computation requires O(nm) time; quantum approaches can potentially improve this.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid applications of quantum network analysis? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Social network influence detection
Protein interaction network clustering
Transportation network optimization
Cryptographic key generation

questions.explanation: Quantum network analysis applies to social networks (influence/centrality), biological networks (community detection), and transportation networks (flow optimization). Cryptographic key generation (d) is a different application of quantum computing not related to graph analysis.
